title: Pneumocystis Jirovecii Pneumonia
oneLiner: Opportunistic fungal pneumonia in immunocompromised (HIV, transplant, steroid therapy) — untreated mortality >90%; treat empirically without delay.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Acute hypoxic respiratory failure** — Rapid desaturation on minimal exertion (drop >5% in SpO₂ on standing/walking) signals severe shunting; immediate high-flow O₂ or CPAP needed.
- **Pneumothorax** — Sudden pleuritic chest pain with tachycardia/hypoxia in a patient with PJP cysts on CT; auscultate for absent breath sounds and get urgent CXR.
- **Concurrent bacterial or CMV co-infection** — Persistent fever despite co-trimoxazole, new focal crackles, or rising CRP; send blood cultures, CMV PCR, and consider broad-spectrum antibiotics.
- **Pulmonary embolism** — Acute-onset pleuritic pain, haemoptysis, unilateral leg swelling; check Wells’ score and D-dimer (beware false positive in inflammation), but CT pulmonary angiogram may be needed if high suspicion.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal:** Subacute onset (days–weeks) of progressive dyspnoea, dry cough, low-grade fever, weight loss.
- **Key exam:** Tachypnoea, tachycardia, hypoxia out of proportion to auscultation (often “clear chest”); fine bibasal crackles in 30–50%.
- **Signs of underlying immunosuppression:** Oral thrush, wasting, lymphadenopathy (HIV), Cushingoid features (chronic steroids), transplant scars.
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Bacterial pneumonia** — Productive cough, focal crackles/consolidation, high CRP; responds to antibiotics.
- **Viral pneumonitis** (CMV, influenza, RSV) — Myalgia, nasal congestion, rapid onset; PCR from BAL/nasopharyngeal swab.
- **Pulmonary oedema** — Orthopnoea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnoea, raised JVP, S3, cardiomegaly on CXR.
- **Pulmonary embolism** — Acute pleuritic pain, haemoptysis, DVT signs; D-dimer/CTPA.
- **Drug-induced pneumonitis** (e.g., methotrexate, amiodarone) — Temporal association with drug, eosinophilia.
- **TB** — Night sweats, weight loss, haemoptysis; apical changes on CXR, positive sputum AFB/NAAT.
- **Splitter:** **Absence of sputum production + profound hypoxia with clear chest on auscultation** → PJP until proven otherwise (vs. bacterial pneumonia with productive cough and focal signs).
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - SpO₂ — target ≥92% (aim >95% if prior respiratory disease).
  - Arterial blood gas — PaO₂, A-a gradient, lactate; PaO₂ <70 mmHg on room air or A-a gradient >35 mmHg indicates severity and need for steroids.
  - ECG — sinus tachycardia; rule out PE (S1Q3T3) or arrhythmia.
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, LFT, CRP, LDH (often markedly raised — >500 U/L suggests PJP).
  - HIV serology (if unknown) + CD4 count (if known HIV+).
  - Serum β-D-glucan (if available) — high sensitivity (90%) and specificity (80%) for PJP; positive strongly supports diagnosis.
  - Blood cultures (×2) and CMV PCR (if suspect co-infection).
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** CXR — bilateral perihilar interstitial infiltrates (bat-wing pattern), often symmetrical; no effusion or cardiomegaly.
  - **Second-line:** High-resolution CT chest — ground-glass opacities, cysts (classic), pneumatoceles; negative CT essentially excludes PJP.
- **Special tests:**
  - **Induced sputum** — for PCR (sensitivity 90%) or silver stain.
  - **Bronchoalveolar lavage (BAL)** — gold standard if induced sputum negative; send for PJP PCR, silver stain, bacterial/fungal/CMV culture.
- **Never-miss test:** **HIV test** (antibody/antigen) — determines treatment duration, need for ART, and prophylaxis intensity. Omission delays lifelong management and exposes others.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Oxygen to target SpO₂ ≥92% (high-flow nasal cannula or non-rebreather mask).
  - If PaO₂ <70 mmHg or A-a gradient >35 mmHg: start **adjunctive prednisolone** 40 mg BD (or IV methylprednisolone 40 mg BD) for 5 days, then taper over 10 days. _Do not wait for bronchoscopy._
  - **Start co-trimoxazole empirically** (see Drugs & Doses) — do not delay for diagnostic confirmation.
  - Consider broad-spectrum antibiotics if features of bacterial co-infection (septic, purulent sputum).
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Assess daily: SpO₂, RR, temperature, CXR if clinically deteriorating.
  - Monitor renal function (co-trimoxazole nephrotoxicity), LFTs, and electrolytes (hyperkalaemia risk).
  - If HIV+: plan for ART initiation once stable (same admission or within 2 weeks).
  - Ensure PJP prophylaxis after treatment completion (see long-term).
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **HIV+ patients:** Start ART within 2 weeks of PJP treatment if no contraindications (beware IRIS). Prophylaxis: co-trimoxazole 960 mg OD (or dapsone if allergy) until CD4 >200 for >3 months.
  - **Non-HIV patients:** Continue prophylaxis for 6–12 weeks if ongoing immunosuppression (e.g., high-dose steroids >20 mg prednisolone/day for >1 month). Re-evaluate need.
  - Respiratory follow-up (spirometry, CT) if residual symptoms or fibrosis.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **IV fluids:** 0.9% NaCl at 1 mL/kg/h (adjust for renal impairment; co-trimoxazole may cause crystalluria — encourage oral fluids if able).
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** LMWH (e.g., enoxaparin 40 mg SC OD) unless contraindicated (thrombocytopenia, active bleeding).
  - **Diet:** NBM only if severely hypoxic or intubation imminent; otherwise full diet with nutritional supplements if cachexic.
  - **Observations:** 4-hourly NEWS2; continuous SpO₂ monitoring if on CPAP or high-flow O₂.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line – Co-trimoxazole (trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole):**
  - Dose: **15–20 mg/kg/day of trimethoprim component** in 3–4 divided doses IV or oral.
  - Typical adult: 240 mg TMP/1200 mg SMX IV QDS (or 3–4 DS tablets [800/160 mg] oral TDS).
  - Duration: **21 days** for proven PJP.
  - Contraindications: G6PD deficiency (check before starting), severe folate deficiency, sulfonamide allergy.
  - Interactions: potentiates warfarin (monitor INR), methotrexate (myelosuppression), hypoglycaemic agents.
- **Common alternatives (if allergy/intolerance):**
  - **Pentamidine** 4 mg/kg IV OD (risk of pancreatitis, hypotension, hyperglycaemia).
  - **Atovaquone** 750 mg BD with fatty meal (mild–moderate disease only).
  - **Primaquine 30 mg OD + Clindamycin 600 mg TDS** (check G6PD before primaquine).
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Co-trimoxazole: **[check local policy]** — reduce dose if CrCl <15 mL/min, or monitor drug levels.
  - Avoid pentamidine in renal impairment.
- **Key adjunctive steroids:**
  - Prednisolone 40 mg BD (or IV methylprednisolone 40 mg BD) for 5 days → taper over 10 days.
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Underdosing co-trimoxazole** — use weight-based dosing, not a single fixed dose. A 50 kg patient needs 750–1000 mg TMP/day, i.e., 4–5 DS tablets per day. A single DS tablet TDS (480 mg TMP/day) is inadequate.
  - **Starting steroids before PJP treatment** — steroids without co-trimoxazole worsen outcome.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Respiratory failure requiring ICU (40% of inpatients); consider CPAP/NIV.
  - Pneumothorax (due to ruptured cysts) — urgent chest drain if tension.
  - Acute kidney injury (co-trimoxazole crystalluria, interstitial nephritis).
  - Pancreatitis (pentamidine).
  - IRIS (immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome) in HIV when ART started soon after PJP — worsening hypoxia, fever.
- **Chronic:**
  - Residual pulmonary fibrosis (spirometric restriction).
  - Relapse if treatment <21 days or inadequate doses.
sections.Prognosis:
- Without treatment: mortality >90%.
- With appropriate treatment in HIV+: 10–20% in-hospital mortality (higher if delay in therapy, severe hypoxia, or co-infection).
- Non-HIV immunocompromised: mortality up to 30–40%.
- Survivors: most return to baseline by 4–6 weeks; some have long-term dyspnoea and exercise limitation.
sections.Pearls (Positive Actions):
- **Start co-trimoxazole on clinical suspicion** — do not wait for bronchoscopy or β-D-glucan result.
- **Check G6PD** before starting co-trimoxazole in patients from high-prevalence ethnic groups (African, Asian, Mediterranean). Use dapsone or atovaquone as alternative.
- **Use high-resolution CT early** if CXR is equivocal — a normal CT virtually excludes PJP.
- **Monitor hydration** — encourage oral fluids or give IV fluids to reduce crystalluria from co-trimoxazole.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Delaying treatment for diagnostic confirmation** — every hour of delay increases mortality.
- **Giving steroids without PJP coverage** — if you start steroids for respiratory failure, always add co-trimoxazole or alternative PJP treatment.
- **Using only oral co-trimoxazole in hypoxic or vomiting patients** — absorption is unreliable; use IV route.
- **Stopping prophylaxis too early** — in HIV, continue until CD4 >200 for >3 months; in non-HIV, continue while immunosuppressed.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - Worsening shortness of breath (cannot walk to toilet).
  - Recurrence of fever >38°C or new productive cough.
  - Chest pain (pleuritic) — concerns pneumothorax.
- **Follow-up:**
  - Infectious diseases clinic within 1 week.
  - Respiratory clinic at 4–6 weeks for spirometry and repeat CXR.
  - HIV clinic (if newly diagnosed) for ART initiation plan.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / ICU if:**
  - SpO₂ <90% on 15 L/min O₂ (FiO₂ >0.6) or requiring CPAP.
  - Respiratory rate >30 despite O₂.
  - pH <7.35 with hypercapnia (PaCO₂ >60 mmHg) on ABG.
  - NEWS2 ≥7 or rising.
  - Haemodynamic instability (SBP <90 mmHg) despite fluids.
  - GCS ≤13 (– consider CO₂ retention or sepsis).
- **Specific thresholds:**
  - PaO₂/FiO₂ ratio <200 (severe ARDS) — immediate ICU referral.
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L — decompensation alert.

rawMarkdown:
## Pneumocystis Jirovecii Pneumonia

**One-liner:** Opportunistic fungal pneumonia in immunocompromised (HIV, transplant, steroid therapy) — untreated mortality >90%; treat empirically without delay.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?

- **Acute hypoxic respiratory failure** — Rapid desaturation on minimal exertion (drop >5% in SpO₂ on standing/walking) signals severe shunting; immediate high-flow O₂ or CPAP needed.
- **Pneumothorax** — Sudden pleuritic chest pain with tachycardia/hypoxia in a patient with PJP cysts on CT; auscultate for absent breath sounds and get urgent CXR.
- **Concurrent bacterial or CMV co-infection** — Persistent fever despite co-trimoxazole, new focal crackles, or rising CRP; send blood cultures, CMV PCR, and consider broad-spectrum antibiotics.
- **Pulmonary embolism** — Acute-onset pleuritic pain, haemoptysis, unilateral leg swelling; check Wells’ score and D-dimer (beware false positive in inflammation), but CT pulmonary angiogram may be needed if high suspicion.

### Presentation

- **Cardinal:** Subacute onset (days–weeks) of progressive dyspnoea, dry cough, low-grade fever, weight loss.
- **Key exam:** Tachypnoea, tachycardia, hypoxia out of proportion to auscultation (often “clear chest”); fine bibasal crackles in 30–50%.
- **Signs of underlying immunosuppression:** Oral thrush, wasting, lymphadenopathy (HIV), Cushingoid features (chronic steroids), transplant scars.

### Differential Diagnosis

- **Bacterial pneumonia** — Productive cough, focal crackles/consolidation, high CRP; responds to antibiotics.
- **Viral pneumonitis** (CMV, influenza, RSV) — Myalgia, nasal congestion, rapid onset; PCR from BAL/nasopharyngeal swab.
- **Pulmonary oedema** — Orthopnoea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnoea, raised JVP, S3, cardiomegaly on CXR.
- **Pulmonary embolism** — Acute pleuritic pain, haemoptysis, DVT signs; D-dimer/CTPA.
- **Drug-induced pneumonitis** (e.g., methotrexate, amiodarone) — Temporal association with drug, eosinophilia.
- **TB** — Night sweats, weight loss, haemoptysis; apical changes on CXR, positive sputum AFB/NAAT.
- **Splitter:** **Absence of sputum production + profound hypoxia with clear chest on auscultation** → PJP until proven otherwise (vs. bacterial pneumonia with productive cough and focal signs).

### Investigations

- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - SpO₂ — target ≥92% (aim >95% if prior respiratory disease).
  - Arterial blood gas — PaO₂, A-a gradient, lactate; PaO₂ <70 mmHg on room air or A-a gradient >35 mmHg indicates severity and need for steroids.
  - ECG — sinus tachycardia; rule out PE (S1Q3T3) or arrhythmia.
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, LFT, CRP, LDH (often markedly raised — >500 U/L suggests PJP).
  - HIV serology (if unknown) + CD4 count (if known HIV+).
  - Serum β-D-glucan (if available) — high sensitivity (90%) and specificity (80%) for PJP; positive strongly supports diagnosis.
  - Blood cultures (×2) and CMV PCR (if suspect co-infection).
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** CXR — bilateral perihilar interstitial infiltrates (bat-wing pattern), often symmetrical; no effusion or cardiomegaly.
  - **Second-line:** High-resolution CT chest — ground-glass opacities, cysts (classic), pneumatoceles; negative CT essentially excludes PJP.
- **Special tests:**
  - **Induced sputum** — for PCR (sensitivity 90%) or silver stain.
  - **Bronchoalveolar lavage (BAL)** — gold standard if induced sputum negative; send for PJP PCR, silver stain, bacterial/fungal/CMV culture.
- **Never-miss test:** **HIV test** (antibody/antigen) — determines treatment duration, need for ART, and prophylaxis intensity. Omission delays lifelong management and exposes others.

### Management

- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Oxygen to target SpO₂ ≥92% (high-flow nasal cannula or non-rebreather mask).
  - If PaO₂ <70 mmHg or A-a gradient >35 mmHg: start **adjunctive prednisolone** 40 mg BD (or IV methylprednisolone 40 mg BD) for 5 days, then taper over 10 days. _Do not wait for bronchoscopy._
  - **Start co-trimoxazole empirically** (see Drugs & Doses) — do not delay for diagnostic confirmation.
  - Consider broad-spectrum antibiotics if features of bacterial co-infection (septic, purulent sputum).
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Assess daily: SpO₂, RR, temperature, CXR if clinically deteriorating.
  - Monitor renal function (co-trimoxazole nephrotoxicity), LFTs, and electrolytes (hyperkalaemia risk).
  - If HIV+: plan for ART initiation once stable (same admission or within 2 weeks).
  - Ensure PJP prophylaxis after treatment completion (see long-term).
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **HIV+ patients:** Start ART within 2 weeks of PJP treatment if no contraindications (beware IRIS). Prophylaxis: co-trimoxazole 960 mg OD (or dapsone if allergy) until CD4 >200 for >3 months.
  - **Non-HIV patients:** Continue prophylaxis for 6–12 weeks if ongoing immunosuppression (e.g., high-dose steroids >20 mg prednisolone/day for >1 month). Re-evaluate need.
  - Respiratory follow-up (spirometry, CT) if residual symptoms or fibrosis.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **IV fluids:** 0.9% NaCl at 1 mL/kg/h (adjust for renal impairment; co-trimoxazole may cause crystalluria — encourage oral fluids if able).
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** LMWH (e.g., enoxaparin 40 mg SC OD) unless contraindicated (thrombocytopenia, active bleeding).
  - **Diet:** NBM only if severely hypoxic or intubation imminent; otherwise full diet with nutritional supplements if cachexic.
  - **Observations:** 4-hourly NEWS2; continuous SpO₂ monitoring if on CPAP or high-flow O₂.

### Drugs & Doses

- **First-line – Co-trimoxazole (trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole):**
  - Dose: **15–20 mg/kg/day of trimethoprim component** in 3–4 divided doses IV or oral.
  - Typical adult: 240 mg TMP/1200 mg SMX IV QDS (or 3–4 DS tablets [800/160 mg] oral TDS).
  - Duration: **21 days** for proven PJP.
  - Contraindications: G6PD deficiency (check before starting), severe folate deficiency, sulfonamide allergy.
  - Interactions: potentiates warfarin (monitor INR), methotrexate (myelosuppression), hypoglycaemic agents.
- **Common alternatives (if allergy/intolerance):**
  - **Pentamidine** 4 mg/kg IV OD (risk of pancreatitis, hypotension, hyperglycaemia).
  - **Atovaquone** 750 mg BD with fatty meal (mild–moderate disease only).
  - **Primaquine 30 mg OD + Clindamycin 600 mg TDS** (check G6PD before primaquine).
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Co-trimoxazole: **[check local policy]** — reduce dose if CrCl <15 mL/min, or monitor drug levels.
  - Avoid pentamidine in renal impairment.
- **Key adjunctive steroids:**
  - Prednisolone 40 mg BD (or IV methylprednisolone 40 mg BD) for 5 days → taper over 10 days.
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Underdosing co-trimoxazole** — use weight-based dosing, not a single fixed dose. A 50 kg patient needs 750–1000 mg TMP/day, i.e., 4–5 DS tablets per day. A single DS tablet TDS (480 mg TMP/day) is inadequate.
  - **Starting steroids before PJP treatment** — steroids without co-trimoxazole worsen outcome.

### Complications

- **Acute:**
  - Respiratory failure requiring ICU (40% of inpatients); consider CPAP/NIV.
  - Pneumothorax (due to ruptured cysts) — urgent chest drain if tension.
  - Acute kidney injury (co-trimoxazole crystalluria, interstitial nephritis).
  - Pancreatitis (pentamidine).
  - IRIS (immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome) in HIV when ART started soon after PJP — worsening hypoxia, fever.
- **Chronic:**
  - Residual pulmonary fibrosis (spirometric restriction).
  - Relapse if treatment <21 days or inadequate doses.

### Prognosis

- Without treatment: mortality >90%.
- With appropriate treatment in HIV+: 10–20% in-hospital mortality (higher if delay in therapy, severe hypoxia, or co-infection).
- Non-HIV immunocompromised: mortality up to 30–40%.
- Survivors: most return to baseline by 4–6 weeks; some have long-term dyspnoea and exercise limitation.

### Pearls (Positive Actions)

- **Start co-trimoxazole on clinical suspicion** — do not wait for bronchoscopy or β-D-glucan result.
- **Check G6PD** before starting co-trimoxazole in patients from high-prevalence ethnic groups (African, Asian, Mediterranean). Use dapsone or atovaquone as alternative.
- **Use high-resolution CT early** if CXR is equivocal — a normal CT virtually excludes PJP.
- **Monitor hydration** — encourage oral fluids or give IV fluids to reduce crystalluria from co-trimoxazole.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)

- **Delaying treatment for diagnostic confirmation** — every hour of delay increases mortality.
- **Giving steroids without PJP coverage** — if you start steroids for respiratory failure, always add co-trimoxazole or alternative PJP treatment.
- **Using only oral co-trimoxazole in hypoxic or vomiting patients** — absorption is unreliable; use IV route.
- **Stopping prophylaxis too early** — in HIV, continue until CD4 >200 for >3 months; in non-HIV, continue while immunosuppressed.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)

- **Return if:**
  - Worsening shortness of breath (cannot walk to toilet).
  - Recurrence of fever >38°C or new productive cough.
  - Chest pain (pleuritic) — concerns pneumothorax.
- **Follow-up:**
  - Infectious diseases clinic within 1 week.
  - Respiratory clinic at 4–6 weeks for spirometry and repeat CXR.
  - HIV clinic (if newly diagnosed) for ART initiation plan.

### Escalation Criteria

- **Call senior / ICU if:**
  - SpO₂ <90% on 15 L/min O₂ (FiO₂ >0.6) or requiring CPAP.
  - Respiratory rate >30 despite O₂.
  - pH <7.35 with hypercapnia (PaCO₂ >60 mmHg) on ABG.
  - NEWS2 ≥7 or rising.
  - Haemodynamic instability (SBP <90 mmHg) despite fluids.
  - GCS ≤13 (– consider CO₂ retention or sepsis).
- **Specific thresholds:**
  - PaO₂/FiO₂ ratio <200 (severe ARDS) — immediate ICU referral.
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L — decompensation alert.
